item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After reading and re-reading I think what this article needs is for you to tackle on it a psychological profile.

Just make a final note of a Foundation analysis team going on about the info they gleaned from the documents. How they piece things in a way to develop better predictors on expected behaviour, future strategies and a map of associates for GAW.

That way you'll make the documents into something that have value to the Foundation and a reason to be in the article.

I love GAW, but in my headcanon they're a valuable source of involuntary information because they're particularly easy to watch, but too hard to actually caught for it to pay off the effort.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am… confused. But pretty funny tho. +1 Gotta read up on my GAW and/or Americans.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to like this, I really do. SCP's work best when they themselves are vehicles for storytelling rather than focusing on describing an anomaly. The backstory here was the main idea for the piece.

The problem is, this goes too far in that direction. Too much exposition and story, barely anything worth securing, containing and protecting for the entity here. A not-completed-yet anomaly is great concept and has the grounds to be interesting, but its just…its so boring as it currently is? You could cut the entire SCP aspect and copy paste the documents into a tale format and it would work just as well if not better. I've seen throwaway items in the anomalous log that were more worth of SCP status.

I think this would be better written if Mr. Bernie Sanders was left explicitly as a present for the Foundation apartment raiders, as a middle finger to them and an open butthole to their face. Anything really to make the entity itself more interesting rather than a weird portal…

-1 but willing to change to +1 if persuaded

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did have the same confusion as some others about why JJ would leave a direct trail to himself somewhere he knew the Foundation might find it.

He's extremely lucky, so as far as he's concerned the Foundation wouldn't be able to track him down directly anyway. Plus, hopefully this comes off, but he's clearly visibly frustrated with, or at least feeling disillusioned (see, my title is still good) with where he is in life, so there's also that slight "Why even bother" feeling too. It's still a relatable feeling even more than two years later, hahaha.

Thank you dearly for reading and commenting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's…not what I was expecting. I did wonder what Bernie Sanders SCP could be made successfully considering 2433 already exists, but I guess that's the same concern JJ had. It feels appropriate to GAW that a part of one of their major ongoing projects is an unfinished and fairly unfunny object that just happens to be anomalous.
I did have the same confusion as some others about why JJ would leave a direct trail to himself somewhere he knew the Foundation might find it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP used to imply serious, mature, smart horror. That's what everybody is here for.

Can only speak for myself here, but I definitely didn't join the site for serious, mature, smart horror.

Additionally, most of the early days stuff was just attempts at horror—most of the serious, mature, and smart horror articles we have today came with the higher expectations that accompanied the more recent years of writing, from what I've seen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
do you realize that you don't have to put these SCPs in your canon… like it's not that hard.

ur yelling at a cloud

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You've forgotten to include "Steely Dan". It's NSFW due to a "marital aid" being front-and-center.

And it dates all the way back to 2008. And it's from the same guy who gave us Procedure 110 Montauk, so… yeah. There were exeptions to "SCP used to imply serious, mature, smart horror" at the start.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This. (NSFW) And this. And this.

These are (or were) very popular on the site, long before this article or even the Gamers Against Weed concept came into existence. Even back then, there were strange, weird, light hearted, or down right over the top piece of literature on this site. To pretend this is somehow something new flies in the face of wiki history. Did the site have an early focus on horror? Definitely, you can't deny that. But to pretend that the wiki was a 100%, totally mature place is in of itself immature.

It's immature to always be mature. Lay back, and have some fun. You may enjoy what you find.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Guess you’ll just have to accept it’s a new era of SCP writing then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Or you could grow up and stop being rude. If you don't like something, downvote it and move on.
If you want more horror, then write more horror. But if you don't write then you've no right to dictate what's allowed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Are you suggesting that the wiki changes the way it works just for you?

This actually

V V V V V

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP used to imply serious, mature, smart horror. That's what everybody is here for. Tens of thousands of people have been wrong before. And i would very much like every article that does not fit the original style of SCP to be clearly labeled, like in the old times, so i can read more intricate horror and less personal reflections and gaping anuses, thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What's the joke here?

EDIT: Or, for that matter, what is so cool about any of this? I mean, I don't want to imply that being disillusioned with people and isolating yourself for days at a time or feeling pangs of self-loathing is particularly cool. I also don't think this is a joke article; none of that is certainly a joke, and just because I inject some light humor into the article with an inanimate humanoid construct that opens its ass doesn't mean the article as a whole then becomes a joke. (There's a lot more silly and better concepts on the Wiki than this that take the same approach anyway.) If humor means an article should be rebranded a -J, and therefore you don't like it, that's your prerogative, though thousands of other voters, and especially tens of thousands of readers, would disagree with this sentiment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel strong disgust towards joke or "cool" SCP's. The reason they are allowed is beyond me, but not being marked with "-J" so i can skip over them - i consider a criminal oversight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One last thing is that the creation of the scp might have been politically motivated, which is something I personally look down upon, no matter what political ideology you have.

You might wish to avoid Gamers Against Weed then; they would not be up your alley much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have had trouble coming up with a proper response to this scp. I find it just completely ridiculous, but not in a very good way. It's a humanoid statue that…pulls it's "buttocks" apart when you say "feel the burn". When I read that part of the article, I almost instantly raised an eyebrow.

All that….aside, while reading the article, I found it pretty underwhelming. I'm not quite sure how to perfectly say why, but the "feel the burn" part was the most interesting part of the article, and that's about it.

One last thing is that the creation of the scp might have been politically motivated, which is something I personally look down upon, no matter what political ideology you have.

Overall, this scp is too simple and uninteresting to be an scp, yet it's a bit too complicated to simply be a non-scp anomalous object in containment/storage.
(-1). I can't say I'm a huge fan of this one.

item.title: Re: What the Title Means
item.content:encoded: I had a very strong feeling the title was referencing the wiki in a meta fashion. Glad to know I wasn't entirely wrong, but also that it was more than that too. Nice work.

item.title: Re: weird personal take
item.content:encoded: I'm happy to hear how much my article's helped you out. More than anything, I write to relate to others and have them relate to my works. <3

item.title: weird personal take
item.content:encoded: I came to reread this scp tonight after quitting twitter and as a result essentially ending or at least curtailing my involvement with a particular wing of that community, one that descended from another online group of some local notoriety. I'm sad but if you can get out and log off, imo, you should. This scp struck me when I first read it, and from the title through to the creator character's naked elation at his perceived success I find it very moving and singularly effective at capturing an element of contemporary culture. It also really helped me out tonight. Thanks.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Man of Memes, this kind of low-effort feedback is against the Criticism Policy and is unnecessary.

Beyond that, the jab at the author was uncalled for, and inflammatory. Please refrain from making posts such as this in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A thing that bends over and spreads its ass when you say "Feel the Bern".
I'm sure the author felt the "burn" when Bernie was screwed out of the primaries.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Had to update it, yet again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean,

Her avatar was Kanaya for a while, so this isn't a exactly a shocker. That aside, I think you're making a bit too much spectacle out of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gog.

DECIBELLES IS A HOMESTUCK DECIBELLES IS A HOMESTUCK DECIBELLES IS A H

*is hit with a brick*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was going to post something relatively lengthy, but TL333S has said everything I wanted to say.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Allready, the moment i saw "site-88" i up-voted, as i think site-88 is cool.
also, i'd climb in his ass. to get to the warehouse

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Link is back up so I reverted back to it. Enjoy the most high-quality video.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Replaced link ;;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The video is gone, unfortunately.

item.title: Re: Translations
item.content:encoded: Thank you for the update! Send my love to all those folks <3

item.title: Re: Translations
item.content:encoded:
To be precise, EFU translated SCP-3015. krt_w, szh_tmh, C-take and I supported him.

But thanks so much! I, as well as many members of JP branch, love GAW and their relatable anomalies of a new age.

item.title: Translations
item.content:encoded:
To promote translations of my work and thank all the authors for their hard work, I am linking to them here in addition to my author page.

  • Chinese by Thermod
  • Japanese by EFU

Thank you all!

item.title: Re: Image Source
item.content:encoded: Turns out, this image wasn't CC-compliant, and Wikimedia Commons has thus taken it down. I will be either looking for a new image of an old man standing outside by the street with cars passing by or, barring that, taking my own picture.
Here's looking at you, kid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: damn this SCP's name is longer than the comment I just wrote right now

item.title: The Post in Which JJ "jockjamsvol6" Has Made Somewhat of a Noticeable Impact on a Youtube Video
item.content:encoded:
+ Show 3 images.
- Hide 3 images.


- Hide 3 images.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't disagree with that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
that is one hell of a title if i say so myself

Would you believe me if I said I couldn't come up with literally any other title for this until I came up with that one? : P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
that is one hell of a title if i say so myself

Joking aside.

Most of the feedback above is my opinion of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gotta plug such a good site, especially when it makes sense for the character.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually like the GAW personalities as a rule, but this feels like a character piece with an item attached almost as an afterthought. I might need to re-read the notes, but this read like "shitpost memes to hide your true feelings," which is fine, but also not a particularly new revelation IRL or regarding GAW.

Also, I get that the internet culture is the reason for GAW's existence in-universe, but dear god, it's strange to come across some weird meme (or meta-meme or meta-meta-meme, or (n*meta)-meme) that I have no context for. OTOH, I can't get mad at something that shills for the WL.

In the end, novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you don't care about the characters enough that you refused to read further, is there anything she could've done differently? There really isn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: wow what a helpful comment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
but not how it was made.

An article detailing how JJ and Jude create a guy who has an extradimensional portal inside of him that leads to a safehouse would be interesting. I invite anyone to write that; I laid down the groundwork here.

So what is the anomalous property of this safehouse? Could this be elaborated on? Is there anything weird about this safehouse besides "This anartist wants the SCP foundation to pay attention to him."

Nothing anomalous about the safehouse itself; that's where JJ has lived his whole life, or like 90% of it. It's the fact that he and lesbian_gengar created a couple of things there that can't really leave it that warrant the building as a site location, until the Foundation can either move them back to their own facilities or find a way around needing to travel back there. Also, JJ isn't an anartist; he's not an artist.

Or maybe it could be JJ himself that's the SCP.

I guess, if you think about it, anyone who has an anomaly or can do reality-bending would be considered an SCP, including Jude and almost all of GAW.

Since SCP-3012 was posted so recently, I'm sort of wondering if there's some sort of theme I should be concerned about.

3012 and this cover a lot of similar themes, but I wanted to go a different route. This is also based very heavily on myself; IDK how much of 3012 kinch based on herself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the worst part about it isn't that it's about memes, but it's about something even less interesting to you than memes.

You have a point there, with the story, even if it does jab at pathos for people like me who are already invested. It doesn't really make sense for the SCP document to contain these anecdotes because they have nothing to do with the anomaly itself. It has brainstorming about what the SCP should be, but not how it was made.

I think it'd be pretty funny if the SCP wasn't the asshole portal mannequin and was instead the place it lead to. "Currently, only AO-███, AO-███, and SCP-████ are contained there due to their anomalous properties." So what is the anomalous property of this safehouse? Could this be elaborated on? Is there anything weird about this safehouse besides "This anartist wants the SCP foundation to pay attention to him." I understand the mister was lazily put together, and making the soulless sexy old man any more interesting would clash with the disillusionment of the anartist but there needs to be something interesting about it. Or maybe it could be JJ himself that's the SCP. I don't know. My mind is easily swayed.

Since SCP-3012 was posted so recently, I'm sort of wondering if there's some sort of theme I should be concerned about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My first question going into an article is "Is the anomaly interesting?" In this case, the answer is a solid no.

Fair enough. He has no soul so he has no personality; that's fine if one doesn't like it.

But once I saw it was just more babbling by the GAW characters, who I can't keep straight and who I don't particularly care about, not to mention there's just so much here, I had no interest in reading further to find out if it was worth my time.

The only thing I get out of this (you can correct me if I'm wrong) is that you didn't read it solely because it had GAW members talking. That's a weird reason to downvote, but it's one that is within the rules and therefore respectable. Though I will reiterate that it is not about memes.

Thank you for the detailed feedback you gave.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're right, I'm sorry. I'll provide actual commentary here.

My first question going into an article is "Is the anomaly interesting?" In this case, the answer is a solid no. I've been trying not to be reactionarily negative to GAW articles — it's here to stay, whether or not I like it — but I still don't like their version of the Misters, just as I don't like the fact that Wondertainment's were all given articles as well. And this is a guy with no personality — quite a few GAW Misters have gotten an upvote from me because of their personalities! — who sucks people into his ass; it's stupid.

Barring that, the next question is, "Is the story being told worth reading?" I mean, addenda and documentation can really make up for a lackluster item or description. But once I saw it was just more babbling by the GAW characters, who I can't keep straight and who I don't particularly care about, not to mention there's just so much here, I had no interest in reading further to find out if it was worth my time.

So this is a swing and a miss, as far as I'm concerned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: tl;dr: It's not about memes. If you want to be a dick, you can at least be a dick and make your critique better than "Meh". See also: http://www.scp-wiki.net/criticism-policy

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since this is about memes, I think it's relevant to sum up my reaction as "tl;dr". -1

item.title: What the Title Means
item.content:encoded:
Since some people have asked. There are a few different meanings behind "A Story Concerning Self-Doubt, Slight Disillusionment, and Frustration, As Told By the Lucky Member of a Group Whose Relatable Works Are Loved Internationally".

The first is literal. This is a story concerning the main character (JJ)'s self-doubt, a man who's only anomaly is 100% incredible luck, disillusionment, and frustration about himself, being a member of Gamers Against Weed, and disconnect for what his friends consider funny. The group's works are also starting to become more notable around the world, and how can't they? A chatroom with a group of internet friends who are millenials making anomalies confounding enough to make the Foundation scratch their heads a bit? Yeah, that'd make people notice what you're doing, especially if you're as politically charged and active as some of these folks.

The second meaning is more metaphorical; this is a personal story about my own self-doubt, disillusionment, and frustration with many aspects of my life, including my friend groups and the SCP Foundation itself. Some days I want to delete every social media account and website account I have and go off-the-grid entirely, and I nearly come close to it at times. Also, I've been lucky enough to have my works translated across various sites. In a sense, my content is both relatable and loved.

The third meaning is a bit more abstract; people have expressed doubt, disillusionment, and frustration with articles and discourse surrounding the GoI Gamers Against Weed. And despite how focused on millenial life and American politics it all is, it's been relatable and beloved by people across the globe and by many age groups. When the Japanese site has translated a number of these works and people you speak to from the different sites say they like this group, regardless of their beliefs or views, you can't help but be amazed. Pretty lucky for the group.

The fourth reason is the most simple and self-serving; Randomini told me once that the more eye-catching an article title is, the more likely people will click on it and read it. So I wanted to have a title eye-catching without it being gibberish.

I think a title is as representative of an article as its content itself, and thus chose it very intentionally. And if I sound pretentious in all this, know that my favorite book is House of Leaves and I've been rereading it for the tenth time recently.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Meh. This still has most of the problems I described in chat, though you fixed the document recovery one.

I guess the biggest one is how disjointed it feels. It gets a lot of ideas out there, but they mostly don't contribute to the whole — which seems to be burdened by the SCP itself, a genuinely funny premise that you should actually run with and leave the documents for a tale or something.

I still dont quite understand the containment procedures, either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's an accurate summation of the skip inside, as I don't want to mislead anyone.

I also listen to a lot of post-rock.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's with the really long titles? I mean, I have no real problem with them, I'm just curious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He, and yes. He is an avid reader of the Library's collection, though he'll downplay it. Because he's so cool, you know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Jockjams is so knowledgeable… because he's a nerd who reads books. Fascinating reveal.

item.title: Image Source
item.content:encoded:
https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Alfred_Petit_2.jpg

This file is licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution-Share Alike 4.0 International license.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm really enjoying the theme of silly-seeming things coupled with the creative people behind them, it gives GAW it's own distinct flavor and I can see reflections of our community in the content. +1

item.title: Author's Post: "In my dreams, I feel that I'm more free."
item.content:encoded:
My first SCP in about a year. Deci Season approaching, fuck whatever y'all been hearing.

Credit to the following people for critique, feedback, and impressions:

  • Doctor Cimmerian first and foremost, for the idea in Roget's channel. This ended up going much differently than what he said, but it was the flame for my inspiration.
  • kinchtheknifeblade
  • Communism will win
  • DolphinSlugchugger
  • Joreth
  • Roget
  • A Random Day
  • Monkeysky
  • fenton-st

Author questions:

  1. When you feel alone in a group of people, how do you cope?
  2. Do you ever feel too disconnected from the rest of the world?
  3. Why did JJ not care that he left his desktop behind?

What I Listened to While Writing: Young Thug's JEFFERY.

EDIT: Also, AO means "Anomalous Object". I can't remember its first usage or how I found it, but I like that parlance.
